description: This red abaca warp ikat tubular skirt worn by women features horizontal bands and geometric designs. It has three panels, horizontally stitched together using red-dyed abaca threads. The upper and lower panels have identical alternating bands of white lines and dashes on red bands, and white chains sandwiched between thin brown bands. The middle panel has distinct zoomorphic patterns with brown bands of geometric designs on both sides. The Bagobos commonly apply this particular design and color motif. Traditionally, the red dye comes from the root of the Morinda citrifolia plant, boiled for days up to two weeks depending on the desired shade.
condition: Good and stable condition, with an indistinct stain on the lower panel. A small patchwork uses abaca thread for stitching, probably a repair or old restoration; the museum team was not permitted to turn the skirt inside out to confirm previous restoration.

images.alt: Red abaca tubular skirt (patadion) mounted on a stand, its horizontal ikat bands of cream lines, dashes, and dark zoomorphic patterns wrapping the cylinder
Reverse of the patadion on its stand, showing the center seam between its two symmetrical woven panels
Detail of the patadion's woven surface, its cream geometric ikat motifs set against the red abaca ground

rights: Images courtesy of the Ethnologisches Museum, Staatliche Museen zu Berlin.
